The natural logarithm

TODO Historical use: made multiplication easier

TODO Geometric picture definition of the hyperbolic logarithm, also called the natural logarithm, as an area.

Multiplication of values corresponds to addition of area. Addition of points on the hyperbola.

Logarithm Taylor Series

The natural logarithm $\ln(x)$ has

Displaying the Taylor polynomials for $\frac{1}{1+x}$ and $\ln(1+x)$, and showing that the integral of the first yields the second. The graph on the right shows the function $\frac{1}{1+x} = 1-x+x^2-x^3+x^4-\ldots$ in black, and its degree-$(d-1)$ approximation $1 - x + x^2 -x^3 + \ldots + (-1)^{d-1}x^{d-1}$ in blue. The area under the curve, shown in gray (and depicted on the left as a black curve), approximates the integral $$\ln(1+x) = \int_0^x \frac{1}{1+t} dt$$ On the right in red is shown the area under the approximation $1 - x + x^2 -x^3 + \ldots + (-1)^{d-1}x^{d-1}$, i.e. the function $x-\frac{x^2}{2} + \frac{x^3}{3} - \ldots + (-1)^{d-1}\frac{x^d}{d}$. This is depicted on the left as a red curve.

By pressing the buttons below, you can increase or decrease the approximation degree $d$. You can also click and drag the black point in the graph on the left to change the $x$-value being approximated, and therefore the limits on integration on the right. Observe that the approximation gets better as $d$ increases if $x$ is between $-1$ and $1$, but otherwise it gets worse!

Area under a polynomial curve
